frameworks.name: convolutional neural network encoding model
frameworks.one_line: CNN trained to predict neural responses to natural images, then used to synthesise RFs.

frameworks.name: nonlinear receptive field characterisation
frameworks.one_line: Generalises RF estimation beyond linear/parametric assumptions to arbitrary nonlinearities.

frameworks.name: simple-cell / complex-cell dichotomy
frameworks.one_line: Classical V1 RF taxonomy used to validate shift-invariance recovered by the method.

core_question: Can a CNN-based encoding model recover nonlinear receptive fields of visual neurons without assuming a specific nonlinearity?
key_assumptions: Neural responses to natural images are predictable from image input via CNN
An image that maximally activates the trained CNN approximates the neuron's RF
No prior parametric form for the nonlinearity is required
manipulations: Type of nonlinearity in simulated cells (shift-invariant, rotation-invariant, etc.)
Dataset source (simulated cells vs. mouse V1 recordings)
key_findings: RF images synthesised from CNN recovered diverse simulated nonlinearities including shift- and rotation-invariant RFs
Applied to mouse V1, the method visualised simple-cell-like and complex-cell-like RFs
Degree of shift-invariance could be quantified from the synthesised RFs
interpretations: CNN-based RF synthesis provides assumption-free nonlinear RF characterisation
Approach may extend to higher visual areas with more complex nonlinearities
